title: Security Architecture
summary: Zero-trust architecture, defence-in-depth, threat modelling (STRIDE), secrets management, and securing microservices.

sections.content:
## Zero-Trust Architecture

Traditional security assumed a trusted network perimeter — inside the firewall is safe. Zero-trust rejects this:

**Never trust, always verify.**

Every request — regardless of network origin — must be authenticated, authorized, and encrypted. No implicit trust based on IP or network location.

### Zero-trust principles:
1. **Verify explicitly** — always authenticate and authorize using all available data
2. **Use least privilege** — limit access to only what's needed
3. **Assume breach** — minimize blast radius, segment access, encrypt everything

sections.title: Traditional Perimeter vs Zero-Trust
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Traditional Perimeter
        FW[Firewall] -->|trusted inside| Internal[All internal services\ntrust each other]
        External[External] -->|blocked| FW
        Attacker[Attacker inside] -->|unrestricted| Internal
    end
    subgraph Zero-Trust
        ZT[Every request verified]
        ZT -->|identity check| Auth[AuthN + AuthZ]
        ZT -->|encrypt all| TLS[mTLS between services]
        ZT -->|least privilege| RBAC[Fine-grained RBAC]
        ZT -->|monitor| SIEM[SIEM + Anomaly Detection]
    end
    style Attacker fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
    style ZT fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff

## Defence in Depth

Multiple overlapping security layers — if one fails, others contain the breach:

```
Layer 1: Network perimeter (firewall, DDoS protection)
Layer 2: Transport (TLS everywhere, mTLS between services)
Layer 3: Authentication (OAuth2/OIDC, MFA)
Layer 4: Authorization (RBAC, ABAC, OPA policies)
Layer 5: Application (input validation, OWASP controls)
Layer 6: Data (encryption at rest, field-level encryption for PII)
Layer 7: Monitoring (anomaly detection, audit logs, SIEM)
```

An attacker who defeats Layer 3 is still stopped by Layer 4.

## Threat Modelling with STRIDE

STRIDE is a framework for identifying security threats:

| Threat | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **S**poofing | Impersonating another user/service | Authentication, mTLS |
| **T**ampering | Modifying data in transit/at rest | HTTPS, message signing, checksums |
| **R**epudiation | Denying actions took place | Audit logs, digital signatures |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | Exposing sensitive data | Encryption, access controls |
| **D**enial of Service | Overwhelming resources | Rate limiting, circuit breakers |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Gaining unauthorized access | Authorization, least privilege |

### Process:
1. Draw a Data Flow Diagram (DFD) of your system
2. Identify trust boundaries (where data crosses security zones)
3. For each component and data flow, apply STRIDE
4. Rate severity (DREAD scoring)
5. Mitigate high-priority threats

sections.title: Microservices Security Layers
sections.content:
graph TB
    Client[Client] -->|TLS + JWT| Gateway[API Gateway\nAuthN + Rate limit]
    Gateway -->|mTLS + JWT validation| SA[Service A]
    Gateway -->|mTLS + JWT validation| SB[Service B]
    SA -->|mTLS| SB
    SA -->|encrypted| DB[(Encrypted DB)]
    SA -->|dynamic secrets| Vault[HashiCorp Vault]
    OPA[OPA Policy Engine] -->|authorization decisions| SA
    OPA -->|authorization decisions| SB
    SIEM[SIEM] <-- |audit events| SA
    SIEM <-- |audit events| SB
    style Gateway fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Vault fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style OPA fill:#059669,color:#fff

rows.Description: Role-based: user has roles, roles have permissions
Attribute-based: decisions based on subject/resource/environment attributes

sections.question: What does 'assume breach' mean in Zero-Trust architecture?
options: Assume that your perimeter firewall will be breached and add more firewall rules
Design security assuming attackers are already inside — minimize blast radius, encrypt internally, monitor for anomalies
Always test your security controls by simulating breaches
Assume that passwords will be leaked and enforce mandatory rotation
sections.explanation: 'Assume breach' is a Zero-Trust principle that says: don't rely on perimeter security to keep attackers out. Instead, assume they're already inside and design accordingly: microsegment networks, encrypt service-to-service traffic, apply least privilege so compromised service X can't access service Y's data, and monitor for anomalous behavior.
